stories.title: Cursor 3.3 - PR Review, Parallel Builds, and Quick Actions
stories.summary: Cursor 0.43 (labeled 3.3 in changelog) ships four distinct features: a redesigned PR review interface with inline comment threads and a commit history view directly inside the editor; parallel agent execution that runs multiple build/task agents concurrently instead of sequentially; an automated change-splitting tool that partitions a large diff into separate PRs; and 'Quick Actions', which let developers pin frequently used agent skills to a toolbar for one-click invocation. Together these changes move Cursor from a single-threaded agentic workflow toward a multi-agent orchestration model while also pulling GitHub PR review out of the browser and into the IDE.
stories.considerations: The parallel agents feature has the most immediate leverage for developers running multi-step plans — for example, when scaffolding a new service that requires simultaneous work on API routes, database migrations, and frontend components, each concern can now be handed to a separate subagent rather than processed in serial, which directly cuts wall-clock time on complex tasks. The PR-splitting tool is an underappreciated addition: teams enforcing small, reviewable PRs often do that splitting manually after the fact, and automating it could meaningfully reduce the friction of working in large codebases with strict review cultures. Both features together signal that Cursor is positioning itself against multi-agent orchestration tools like Devin and SWE-agent, targeting the 'autonomous large task' use case that single-context LLM sessions handle poorly. The main gotcha is agent coordination overhead — parallel subagents operating on the same codebase can produce conflicting edits if their scopes aren't cleanly separated, and it's unclear from the changelog whether Cursor enforces file-level locking or leaves conflict resolution to the developer. Quick Actions are a quality-of-life win but represent a workflow lock-in vector: teams that build muscle memory around pinned skills will find migration to other tools increasingly costly.

stories.title: Fast and Comprehensive Code Review in Windsurf
stories.summary: Windsurf has added two code review features — Devin Review and Quick Review — that run directly inside the IDE rather than requiring a context switch to a separate tool like GitHub's PR interface. Devin Review appears to target comprehensive, agent-driven analysis (likely leveraging Windsurf's AI agent infrastructure to inspect code more deeply), while Quick Review offers a faster, lighter-weight pass for rapid feedback. Both features bring review into the same workspace where code is written, eliminating the round-trip between editor and external review tooling. The core problem solved is fragmentation: developers previously had to leave their editor to initiate or read code reviews, breaking flow.
stories.considerations: The most immediate workflow impact is on solo developers or small teams doing self-review before pushing — instead of opening a draft PR just to get AI feedback, a developer can trigger Quick Review inline after finishing a feature branch, catching issues before the commit even leaves the machine. A less obvious use case is using Devin Review as a pre-merge gate in trunk-based development workflows where formal PR reviews are lightweight or skipped, effectively substituting automated deep review for human review cycles. This matters now because AI-assisted review is maturing past 'linting plus suggestions' into something closer to a junior reviewer, and the bottleneck has shifted from review quality to review friction — keeping it in-editor directly attacks that friction. The main gotcha is that 'comprehensive' AI review still has well-known blind spots around business logic, security edge cases, and intent — developers risk over-trusting in-editor review and skipping human review entirely, which would be a regression. There's also no detail yet on how Devin Review handles large diffs or monorepo-scale PRs, which is where review tooling typically degrades fastest.

stories.title: Claude Code Hooks: Intercept and Control Every Step of the Agent Loop
stories.summary: Claude Code Hooks introduces a lifecycle interception system that lets developers attach shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts to specific agent loop events — SessionStart, PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, and SessionEnd. Mechanically, Claude Code serializes the event context as JSON and pipes it to your registered handler via stdin, then reads the handler's stdout to determine whether to proceed, block, or retry. This turns hooks into active decision gates, not passive observers: a PreToolUse hook can outright block a tool call before it executes, a PermissionDenied hook can trigger a retry, and a Stop hook can run a secondary LLM evaluation against multi-criteria quality checks before a turn actually concludes. Developers can now enforce policies, audit trails, and quality gates programmatically without forking Claude Code or relying on prompt engineering alone.
stories.considerations: The most immediate high-value scenario is enforcing write-safety in production-adjacent environments: a PreToolUse hook on file-write or shell-exec tool calls can check whether the target path is in an allowlist, blocking destructive operations before they happen — something previously only achievable through careful system prompt wording that Claude could still reason around. Beyond safety, this opens up non-obvious use cases like telemetry pipelines (pipe every tool call to a logging endpoint for compliance auditing), dynamic context injection at SessionStart (fetch the latest feature flags or DB schema and prepend them to context), or chaining a cheaper model as a quality-gate evaluator on Stop to avoid shipping half-baked outputs. This matters now because agentic coding sessions are getting longer and more autonomous — the gap between 'Claude does something unexpected' and 'a human notices' is widening, and hooks are the missing control plane for that gap. The main gotcha is latency: every synchronous hook adds wall time to the agent loop, so LLM-backed hooks on high-frequency events like PostToolUse can meaningfully slow multi-step sessions — profile your hook handlers before deploying them on anything with tight turnaround requirements.
